topic: Membach SG null (1999 eclipse) — credit-claiming both detection and null
change_summary: detail_evidence rewrite (999→1961, +96%); detail_verdict_text expanded (168→1131, +560%) with post-hoc-test paragraph and EM-trilemma argument added since c2 (April 8 2026).
our_argument_summary: WIN-013 is classified Misleading because the dome claims credit for both a positive gravity detection (WIN-011, Mohe spring gravimeter) and a null result (WIN-013, Membach SGs) without advance specification of which sites should show effects. The detail_evidence now adds (a) an instrumental hierarchy argument — Membach SGs ~1,000× more sensitive than Mohe LaCoste-Romberg, so the precise instrument's null overrides the crude one's marginal positive — and (b) a three-horn trilemma against the dome's 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' escape: EM interpretation contradicts WIN-011 spring-gravimeter detection; an EM interpretation forces κ's denominator (Δg) to zero, making WIN-012's coupling constant undefined; and any location-dependent escape lacks advance specification, preserving unfalsifiability.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-013 is not validated by monitor.py. Domain 7 in monitor.py handles the WIN-011/012 coupling constant cluster only — not WIN-013 or WIN-014. There is no fetch of Van Camp 2001 data, no comparison logic, and no expected/observed pair for WIN-013. The dome's '0.0 μGal CONFIRMED' status is an editorial declaration in 02-wins.txt with no automated check.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither — there is no validation logic at all. The WIN exists only as a text entry asserting Van Camp 1999 found 0.0 μGal.
data_sources_used: None within monitor.py — no API call, no static fetch, no comparison.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome reframes the null as 'electromagnetic, not gravitational,' which functions as an interpretive escape for any null gravimeter measurement. No code-level adaptive tolerance is involved because there is no code-level check.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The current code_analysis.monitoring='none' tag (corrected from 'hardcoded' per c2 finding) matches what monitor.py actually does — i.e., nothing — and is consistent with WIN-014's tag.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: grep of monitor.py and pull_data.py finds zero hits for 'membach', 'van camp', '013', or any related identifier. Domain 7 (the eclipse coupling block) covers only WIN-011/012; the only 0.0 μGal that appears anywhere is in 02-wins.txt narrative text. With no fetch and no comparison, the correct tag is 'none' — confirming the c2 correction.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics predicts no eclipse gravity signal; SG nulls are the expected outcome under standard physics. The dome model is not relabeling a standard explanation here — it is post-hoc credit-claiming an expected null and re-framing it as 'EM, not gravitational.' That re-framing is dome-specific (and self-contradictory with WIN-011/012), not a relabel of an existing standard explanation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Van Camp et al.'s eclipse gravimetry result was published in 2001 (DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637) — five years before the dome model existed. The dome cites 'Van Camp 1999' (the eclipse year) which obscures the publication-vs-eclipse timeline. The 0.0 μGal value was indexed and citable long before any dome 'prediction' commit.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome equation predicts the Membach SG should report 0.0 μGal. Disc radius, firmament height, aetheric refraction index n(r), and the V12 coordinate function play no role in producing the 0.0 number. The value is adopted from Van Camp 2001 directly.

holes_found.description: The '~1,000× greater sensitivity' figure is at the upper end of what the published noise floors actually support. Modern superconducting gravimeters achieve ~0.01 μGal (10 nGal) noise floors over relevant integration times; LaCoste-Romberg D spring gravimeters reach ~1 μGal short-term noise (somewhat looser over hours due to drift). The honest sensitivity ratio is closer to 100× short-term and 500× when long-period drift is included. '1,000×' is defensible only if you compare best-case nGal SG long-integration noise to worst-case spring drift over hours, which is an apples-to-oranges comparison a dome defender will exploit.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to 'two to three orders of magnitude more precise' or 'roughly 100–500× more sensitive depending on integration time and drift,' and cite the noise-floor numbers explicitly (Hinderer et al. 2007 SG specs vs. LaCoste-Romberg D datasheet) so the figure is bounded by sources rather than rhetorical.

holes_found.description: The trilemma's clause (c) — the location-dependent escape — is dispatched in one sentence ('the model avoids contradiction by asserting different sites respond differently, but provides no advance specification of which sites should show effects'). A sophisticated dome defender will fill this gap with a post-hoc story: subsurface conductivity differs (Mohe sits over basement granite, Membach over Devonian limestone with different magnetic susceptibility), so the EM-coupling response differs by site. We don't address this defense, and it is the strongest version of the location-dependent escape.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence to clause (c): 'A defender might attribute the difference to subsurface conductivity (basement granite at Mohe vs. Devonian carbonate at Membach), but the dome's published WIN list and monitor.py contain no subsurface-conductivity term, no per-site coupling tensor, and no advance prediction tying expected response to any site-specific geophysical parameter. The escape is post-hoc rationalization, not a model output.' This converts a hand-wave into a documented gap in the dome's published equations.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence prose still references 'Van Camp et al., 2001' inline but the dome's 02-wins.txt source line says 'Van Camp 1999.' We currently note this in the c2 citation_check but the user-visible WIN-013 panel doesn't surface the year discrepancy. A reader who checks the dome's WIN list and our review side by side will see two different years and may not know who is right.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a parenthetical: 'The dome cites Van Camp 1999 (the eclipse year); the underlying paper appeared in 2001, five years after the eclipse — making the dome's 1999 attribution impossible to reconcile with publication history.' This makes the timeline explicit on the panel itself.

holes_found.description: The detail_verdict_text now ends with the boilerplate post-hoc paragraph used across many WINs ('the confirming value comes from a field-specific published dataset...'). For WIN-013 specifically, the relevant dataset is gravimetry/eclipse data — calling it 'IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.' as the boilerplate does is generic and does not name the actual Van Camp 2001 source. This is a small but visible boilerplate-fit issue: the post-hoc paragraph reads as templated rather than tailored.
holes_found.recommendation: Specialize the boilerplate sentence for WIN-013: replace the dataset list with 'the Van Camp et al. 2001 Membach SG eclipse measurement (DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637), publicly indexed since 2001 — five years before the dome model existed.' This costs nothing rhetorically and removes a clear template-fit signature.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text together exceed 3,000 characters. WIN-013's argument is fundamentally the same as WIN-014's (same logical structure, different eclipse), and a reader who reads both back-to-back encounters near-redundant prose. Not a fix-this-now issue, but flag for a future tightening pass.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider promoting the cross-WIN unfalsifiability argument to Section 1.x prose where it is made once and cited from WIN-013/014 panels, leaving the panels with site-specific details only. Defer to a holistic review of the eclipse-gravity cluster.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's 'EM not gravitational' interpretation, taken seriously, creates an ADDITIONAL contradiction with WIN-015 (Meyl scalar wave Faraday penetration). If the eclipse effect is electromagnetic, then any conducting Faraday cage should attenuate it. Membach is in a deep, low-noise underground vault; Mohe is at surface. EM screening alone could explain the difference — but that's the standard-physics explanation for a NULL effect, not a dome-confirmation. We can use the dome's own EM interpretation against itself: under EM, the dome's preferred mechanism explains nothing the standard model doesn't, and the differential response is just shielding.
sources: Hampel et al. 2003 (Membach vault EM specs)
WIN-015 detail_evidence on scalar-wave penetration claims

stronger_arguments.description: The instrumental hierarchy argument can be sharpened by citing the Yang & Wang (2002) reanalysis of the Mohe data, which the dome already references in WIN-011's detail_evidence. That reanalysis attributed the Mohe anomaly timing to instrumental drift correlated with environmental temperature — making the SG null at Membach not just 'more precise' but 'consistent with the corrected Mohe re-analysis.' Connecting WIN-013 to the Mohe reanalysis closes the loop more tightly than 'SGs are more sensitive.'
sources: Yang & Wang (2002) reanalysis cited in WIN-011 detail_evidence

kernel_of_truth.description: Membach SGs really did record a null during the August 1999 eclipse, and SG nulls are a real empirical constraint on Allais-effect-style claims. Citing the Membach result is not fabrication — Van Camp et al. (2001) is a real paper with a real null. The dome's mistake is interpreting 'null' as 'confirmation of dome model' rather than 'confirmation of standard physics's prediction that there is no eclipse gravity anomaly.'
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: A null result that is exactly what standard physics predicts cannot be evidence FOR a model that proposes an eclipse-induced effect. The dome's escape — calling it 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' — is a re-framing that contradicts WIN-011 (where the same eclipse phenomenon IS gravitational) and zeros out WIN-012's coupling constant. The kernel of truth (Membach null is real) leaves the dome with three options that all break other WINs.

cross_win_consistency.notes: The four-WIN cluster is now internally self-consistent: WIN-011 (Not Demonstrated, hardcoded), WIN-012 (Self-Contradicted, hardcoded — explicitly cites the κ-vanishes argument), WIN-013 (Misleading, monitoring='none'), WIN-014 (Misleading, monitoring='none'). Code_analysis tags match monitor.py reality (Domain 7 covers only WIN-011/012). The argumentative structure is shared across WIN-013 and WIN-014 with site-specific details swapped — appropriate for parallel WINs. WIN-013 detail_evidence cites the κ-undefined consequence, which is the same argument WIN-012 uses; the cross-references reinforce rather than contradict. One small drift point: WIN-013 finding line says 'WIN-011's positive detection' but WIN-011 has verdict 'Not Demonstrated' (we contest the positive detection). The framing 'positive detection' is rhetorically grants the dome's claim for the unfalsifiability argument — fine, but a reader skim-checking the verdict table may notice the apparent tension. Not flagging as a hole because the qualifier is clear inside the trilemma.

claims_checked: κ = 10.9 nT / 6.5 μGal = 1.677 nT/μGal (matches dome's stated 1.67 nT/μGal to 0.4%)
If Δg = 0.0 μGal, κ = 10.9/0 → undefined (matches our argument)
'~1,000× greater sensitivity' — bounds: 100× (1 μGal LR-D vs 0.01 μGal SG short-term) to 500× (5 μGal LR-D drift vs 0.01 μGal SG long-term integration). 1,000× is at the optimistic edge.
Van Camp et al. 2001, DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 — DOI prefix 10.1023 corresponds to Springer/Kluwer; year correctly stated as 2001 (eclipse year was 1999).
quantitative_verification.computations: Hand-checked κ arithmetic via Python (10.9/6.5 = 1.6769); confirmed division-by-zero under WIN-013/014 null. Sensitivity ratio bounded against published instrument noise floors.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Only the '1,000×' figure sits at the upper boundary of justifiable sensitivity ratios — flagged in holes_found as moderate. All other numerical claims check out.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender, accepting our six methodological principles, would argue: 'You are reading WIN-011 and WIN-013 as joint claims about the same physical quantity, but the dome model treats them as joint constraints on a TWO-component effect — gravitational at Mohe (where surface conditions favor mass-coupling), electromagnetic at Membach (where the deep underground vault and SG instrumentation isolates the magnetic mode). The model's prediction is not 'all sites show gravity' but 'sites with surface-coupling show Δg, sites with vault-isolation show Δg=0 because the vault filters the gravity-coupled component while leaving the magnetic anomaly unaffected.' That is a falsifiable two-component prediction: any site with vault isolation that DID show Δg ≠ 0 would falsify it. The Wang 2000 Mohe positive and Van Camp 2001 Membach null are jointly consistent with the two-component model; they would be inconsistent only if both sites had been instrumented identically, which they were not. Your unfalsifiability charge requires the model to predict the same outcome at both sites, which it does not.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add a sentence to detail_evidence (after the trilemma) that closes this two-component escape: 'Even granting a site-dependent two-component framing, the dome model never publishes a vault-isolation threshold, a coupling-mode partition function, or any quantitative rule for which instruments at which sites should report Δg vs Δg = 0. The two-component story is constructed after Mohe and Membach were observed, not before — it is a curve fit to two data points and contains no advance prediction for any future eclipse measurement at any site.' This converts the strongest defense into a documented absence in the dome's published equations and timestamps.

deeper_analysis: The detail_evidence rewrite since c2 substantially strengthened WIN-013 by adding the explicit EM-trilemma. The c2 review's two moderate holes (monitoring tag mismatch and EM-escape not addressed) are both resolved. Cross-WIN consistency with WIN-011/012/014 is now clean. The remaining attack surface is rhetorical at the margins: the '1,000×' sensitivity figure is upper-bound, the location-dependent escape needs one more sentence to fully neutralize the subsurface-conductivity dodge, and the boilerplate post-hoc paragraph reads templated. The current verdict (Misleading) is correct and should hold; recommended_action stays at minor_edit. None of the remaining holes are critical — they are tightenings that would harden the section against the strongest dome-defender rebuttal, not corrections to factual error.

citations_verified: Wang et al. 2000, Phys. Rev. D 62 041101, DOI 10.1103/PhysRevD.62.041101 — referenced for Mohe spring gravimeter (verified in c2 and re-checked).
Van Camp et al. 2001, DOI 10.1023/A:1013940928637 — referenced for Membach SG null (DOI prefix 10.1023 confirms Kluwer/Springer; year 2001 correct).
citations_unchecked: Hampel et al. 2003 (Membach vault EM specs) — proposed in stronger_arguments, not yet cited in our text. Would need verification before adding.
Yang & Wang (2002) Mohe reanalysis — proposed strengthening, not currently in WIN-013 text but exists in WIN-011's detail_evidence per cross-reference.
